lessons.title: Replace CSS ID Selectors with getByRole for Button Locators
lessons.context: CSS id selectors like #btn-search are fragile — they break when developers rename IDs during refactoring. getByRole() locators are tied to the element's semantic role and accessible name, which are far more stable and also validate accessibility compliance.

problem.cause: CSS id selectors are implementation details that change frequently during development

antipattern.description: Using CSS id selectors for button locators
antipattern.why_bad: IDs are implementation details — renamed during refactoring, causing test failures unrelated to functionality

solution.description: Use getByRole() with the button's accessible name. Stable across refactoring and validates accessibility.

lessons.ai_instruction: When generating button locators, always prefer getByRole('button', { name: '...' }) over CSS id selectors. Only use getByTestId() when the button has no meaningful accessible name.

lessons.title: Use filter({ hasText }) Instead of nth()/first() for List Item Selection
lessons.context: Selecting list items by index (nth(0), first()) is brittle — the order of items can change due to sorting, filtering, or new data. Selecting by content with filter({ hasText }) targets the specific item regardless of its position.

problem.error: Test selects wrong item after list order changes
problem.cause: Index-based selection breaks when list order changes

antipattern.description: Using nth() or first() to select a specific item from a list
antipattern.why_bad: List order is not guaranteed — nth(0) today may be a different item tomorrow
code: // BAD — index-based, breaks when order changes

solution.description: Use filter({ hasText }) to select by content, making the locator resilient to order changes.
code: // GOOD — content-based, resilient to order changes

lessons.ai_instruction: When selecting a specific item from a list or table, always use .filter({ hasText: '...' }) to target by content. Never use .nth() or .first() unless position is semantically meaningful.

lessons.title: Inline HTML Mock via page.setContent() Enables Fast Isolated UI Tests
lessons.context: For systems where a real backend is not available, page.setContent() can inject inline HTML directly into the browser. Centralizing the HTML mock in the Page Object's setupMockPage() method keeps spec files clean and makes the mock reusable across all test cases.

problem.error: Tests require a running backend server
problem.cause: Tests navigate to real URLs that require a running backend

antipattern.description: Duplicating inline HTML mock in every test case
antipattern.why_bad: HTML mock duplicated across tests is hard to maintain — one change requires updating every test
code: // BAD — HTML mock duplicated in every test

solution.description: Centralize the HTML mock in the Page Object's setupMockPage() method.

lessons.ai_instruction: When generating tests that use inline HTML mocks, always centralize the HTML in a setupMockPage() method in the Page Object. Never duplicate HTML mock content across test cases.
